lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.

My ignorance of many natural phenomena is no excuse for your ignorance as to the origin of souls. You ought, according to your boasting dream to know everything. The thing of most importance was forgotten in your cargo of Eastern wares.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You press me to give my opinions about the nature of things.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If there were room, I could repeat to you the views of Lucretius who follows Epicurus, or those of Aristotle as taught by the Peripatetics, or of Plato and Zeno by the Academics and the Stoics.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Passing to the church, where we have the rule of truth, the books of Genesis and the Prophets and Ecclesiastes, give us much information on questions of this kind.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But if we profess ignorance about all these things, as also about the origin of souls, you ought in your Apology to acknowledge your ignorance of all alike, and to ask your calumniators why they had the impudence to force you to reply on this single point when they themselves know nothing of all those great matters.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But Oh! how vast was the wealth contained in that trireme which had come full of all the wares of Egypt and the East to enrich the poverty of the city of Rome.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Thou who alone by writing sav’st the state.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Unless you had come from the East, that very learned man would be still sticking fast among the mathematici, and all Christians would still be ignorant of what might be said against fatalism.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You have a right to ply me with questions about astrology and the cause of the sky and the stars, when you brought to land a ship full of such wares as these.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I acknowledge my poverty; I have not grown rich to this extent in the East like you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You learned in your long sojourn under the shadow of the Pharos what Rome never knew: Egypt instructed you in lore which Italy did not possess till now.
